question: What corpus would definitively distinguish H5a from H5b?
requirements: A text with KNOWN composition date
Both versions segmentable by the same textual unit
Documented transmission history between the two versions

candidates.pre_canon_source: Birmingham manuscript (~568-645 CE), Codex Parisino-Petropolitanus (~640-680 CE)
candidates.status: Manuscript images available; no morphologically-tagged digital corpus exists
candidates.effort: Requires Arabic NLP pipeline + manual verification

candidates.pre_canon_source: LXX (~250 BCE, Greek translation from pre-Masoretic Hebrew)
candidates.status: CATSS and Rahlfs LXX are digitally available
candidates.effort: Moderate: LXX is in Greek (translation), not Hebrew. Comparing H across languages introduces confounds. Still, if LXX Greek Isaiah has similar H to DSS Hebrew Isaiah, it would support H5a.
candidates.caveat: LXX is a TRANSLATION, not a copy. Language change affects H independently of transmission.

candidates.status: Digital texts available (various quality); some morphological tagging exists
candidates.effort: Moderate: same language (Hebrew), independent transmission lines, both pre-date final MT canonization
candidates.note: This is the STRONGEST candidate: same language, same text, two independent transmission lines diverging ~400 BCE. If both show similar H, it confirms H5a (structure predates the split). If MT shows higher H, it supports H5b.

candidates.pre_canon_source: Greek NT manuscripts (P66, P75, Codex Sinaiticus)
candidates.status: Both digitally available with morphological tagging
candidates.effort: Cross-language comparison (Greek→Latin translation), same caveats as LXX

recommendation: The Samaritan Pentateuch vs Masoretic Pentateuch comparison is the most promising: same language, same base text, independent transmission since ~400 BCE. If both show similar H, H5a is confirmed (structure predates the divergence). If MT shows higher H, H5b gains support. A tagged digital Samaritan Pentateuch would resolve the question.
